msgs.text:
Turn 2 — Stage A hardened into compile packets The attached lane instructions ask for an inline Lean 4/mathlib implementation plan, using the coding agent plus the 100-CPU remote Lean backend, not for carrying out the formalization itself.The problem lane is specifically to formalize correctness of the Q₂ presentation in current_manuscript.tex / .pdf.

Turn 1 established the finite-quotient-first architecture and ended with the next task: start Stage A packets 1–4: Gen, word evaluation, finite ω₂, twoCore, and AdmissibleMarkedQuotient.The key Turn 2 adjustment is structural: do not prove the four displayed words by ad hoc rewrites. Build one uniform word-evaluation and quotient/product functoriality layer, then every later relation, quotient, subdirect product, and finite-target statement becomes an instance of that layer.The official problem’s submission convention also reinforces this choice: the first two generators are the tame generators, later generators must lie in the relevant p-core, and profinite exponents are serialized only for finite verification modulo a fixed modulus.The manuscript’s theorem uses generators σ, τ, x₀, x₁, right conjugation, the auxiliary words σ₂, uᵢ, d₀, z₀, c₀, g₀, d_g, h_c, h₀, the tame relation τ^σ = τ², and the wild relation h₀ u₁⁻¹ x₁^σ c₀ = 1; it also states that for maps to finite groups the pro-2 normal-closure condition is equivalent to x₀, x₁ ∈ O₂(G).

1. Stage A target after Turn 2 The immediate deliverable should be a small compiling foundation with these theorem interfaces:lean namespace Q2Presentation inductive Gen | sigma | tau | x0 | x1 deriving DecidableEq, Fintype, Repr abbrev Marking (G : Type*) [Group G] := Gen → G def rconj [Group G] (x g : G) : G := g⁻¹ * x * g def comm [Group G] (x y : G) : G := x⁻¹ * y⁻¹ * x * y/-- Finite target interpretation of the profinite idempotent ω₂. -/def powOmega2 [Group G] [Fintype G] (g : G) : G := ...theorem map_powOmega2 {G H : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] [Group H] [Fintype H](φ : G →* H) (g : G) :φ (powOmega2 g) = powOmega2 (φ g) := ...theorem powOmega2_eq_one_of_odd_order {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] {g : G}(hodd : Odd (orderOf g)) :powOmega2 g = 1 := ...theorem powOmega2_eq_self_of_two_power_order {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] {g : G}(h2 : ∃ n, orderOf g = 2^n) :powOmega2 g = g := ...theorem powOmega2_idempotent {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] (g : G) :powOmega2 (powOmega2 g) = powOmega2 g := ...def twoCore (G : Type*) [Group G] [Fintype G] : Subgroup G := ...theorem twoCore_normal (G : Type*) [Group G] [Fintype G] :(twoCore G).Normal := ...theorem normal_pSubgroup_le_twoCore {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G](N : Subgroup G) (hN : N.Normal)(h2 : IsPGroup 2 N) :N ≤ twoCore G := ...theorem mem_twoCore_iff_normalClosure_is_twoGroup {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] {x : G} :x ∈ twoCore G ↔ IsPGroup 2 (Subgroup.normalClosure ({x} : Set G)) := ...theorem pair_mem_twoCore_iff_pair_normalClosure_is_twoGroup {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] {x y : G} :x ∈ twoCore G ∧ y ∈ twoCore G ↔ IsPGroup 2 (Subgroup.normalClosure ({x, y} : Set G)) := ...def tameRel {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] (q : Marking G) : Prop := ...def wildRel {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] (q : Marking G) : Prop := ...def generates {G : Type*} [Group G] (q : Marking G) : Prop :=Subgroup.closure (Set.range q) = ⊤ def Admissible {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] (q : Marking G) : Prop :=generates q ∧ tameRel q ∧ wildRel q ∧q Gen.x0 ∈ twoCore G ∧ q Gen.x1 ∈ twoCore G theorem admissible_map_surjective {G H : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] [Group H] [Fintype H](φ : G →* H) (hφ : Function.Surjective φ){q : Marking G} :Admissible q → Admissible (fun a => φ (q a)) := ...theorem admissible_subdirect {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι]{G : ι → Type*} [∀ i, Group (G i)] [∀ i, Fintype (G i)](q : ∀ i, Marking (G i))(h : ∀ i, Admissible (q i)) :Admissible (diagonalSubdirectMarking q) := ...end Q2Presentation This is the Stage A nucleus. Once these compile, Lemma 2.1 and the finite part of Lemma 2.2 become formal consequences rather than bespoke proofs. That is the structural pattern that prevents getting stuck in one special quotient.

2. File layout for the first compile sprint Use smaller files than Turn 1 suggested. The first sprint should avoid importing local fields, Galois theory, profinite groups, or cohomology. Stage A is finite group algebra only.Q2Presentation/Foundation/Gen.lean Q2Presentation/Foundation/Omega2Residue.lean Q2Presentation/Foundation/Omega2Power.lean Q2Presentation/Foundation/Expr.lean Q2Presentation/Foundation/Q2Words.lean Q2Presentation/Foundation/TwoCore.lean Q2Presentation/Foundation/Admissible.lean Q2Presentation/Foundation/Subdirect.lean

The dependency order should be:Gen↓ Omega2Residue → Omega2Power↓ Expr → Q2Words↓ TwoCore↓ Admissible↓ Subdirect Expr is the important new design choice. Define a tiny syntax tree for the Q₂ words and prove eval_map once. That gives relation preservation under quotients and products for both relators uniformly.lean inductive GExpr | one : GExpr | gen : Gen → GExpr | mul : GExpr → GExpr → GExpr | inv : GExpr → GExpr | powNat : GExpr → ℕ → GExpr | powOmega2 : GExpr → GExpr deriving Repr namespace GExpr def eval {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] :GExpr → Marking G → G | one, _ => 1 | gen a, q => q a | mul u v, q => eval u q * eval v q | inv u, q => (eval u q)⁻¹ | powNat u n, q => (eval u q)^n | powOmega2 u, q => Q2Presentation.powOmega2 (eval u q)theorem eval_map {G H : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] [Group H] [Fintype H](φ : G →* H) (w : GExpr) (q : Marking G) :φ (eval w q) = eval w (fun a => φ (q a)) := by induction w <;> simp [eval, *, map_powOmega2]end GExpr

Then the displayed words are data, not proofs:lean namespace Q2Word open GExpr Gen def sigma : GExpr := gen .sigma def tau : GExpr := gen .tau def x0 : GExpr := gen .x0 def x1 : GExpr := gen .x1 def rconjExpr (x g : GExpr) : GExpr :=mul (mul (inv g) x) g def commExpr (x y : GExpr) : GExpr :=mul (mul (mul (inv x) (inv y)) x) y def sigma2 : GExpr := powOmega2 sigma def u0 : GExpr := powOmega2 (mul x0 tau)def u1 : GExpr := powOmega2 (mul x1 tau)def d0 : GExpr := mul u0 (inv x0)def z0 : GExpr := rconjExpr x0 sigma2 def c0 : GExpr := commExpr d0 z0 def g0 : GExpr := powNat sigma2 2 def dg : GExpr := rconjExpr d0 g0 def hc : GExpr := commExpr dg d0 def h0 : GExpr :=mul (mul (mul (mul (mul (rconjExpr x0 g0) x0) dg) d0)(powNat d0 2)) hc def tameRelator : GExpr :=mul (rconjExpr tau sigma) (inv (powNat tau 2))def wildRelator : GExpr :=mul (mul (mul h0 (inv u1)) (rconjExpr x1 sigma)) c0 end Q2Word That is intentionally boring. The sophistication belongs in eval_map, powOmega2, and twoCore, not in handwritten simplification of h₀.

3. Finite ω₂: choose the elementwise route Do not use Appendix C’s serialized integer as the definition. The manuscript explicitly says the finite computation is independent and that the proof itself uses the genuine profinite exponent.Appendix C can become a regression test later, not a proof dependency.

The clean formal route is elementwise:

For a finite group element g, let n = orderOf g, write n = 2^a * m, Odd m.Define omega2Residue n as the unique residue modulo n satisfying omega2Residue n ≡ 1 mod 2^a omega2Residue n ≡ 0 mod m.Thenlean powOmega2 g := g ^ omega2Residue (orderOf g)The load-bearing theorem is not the CRT existence lemma itself. The load-bearing theorem is compatibility under divisibility:lean theorem omega2Residue_compat_of_dvd {m n : ℕ} (hm : m ∣ n) :omega2Residue n ≡ omega2Residue m [MOD m] := ...From that, map_powOmega2 follows because orderOf (φ g) divides orderOf g.

This gives four future uses for free:lean map_powOmega2 -- quotient/product functoriality powOmega2_eq_one_of_odd_order -- tame quotient simplification powOmega2_eq_self_of_two_power_order -- pro-2 quotient simplification powOmega2_idempotent -- σ₂ and uᵢ normalization lemmas This is the right abstraction boundary. It closes many later cases at once: tame quotient, pro-2 quotient, finite semidirect coefficient groups, and Heisenberg/extraspecial coefficient groups all use the same lemma.

4. twoCore: define it for theorem power, not for convenience

There are two plausible definitions:lean -- Option A def pCore := ⨅ P : Sylow p G, P.toSubgroup -- Option B def pCore := sSup {N : Subgroup G | N.Normal ∧ IsPGroup p N}Run both as parallel compile experiments. The winner is the one that makes these four statements shortest:lean theorem pCore_normal ...theorem pCore_isPGroup ...theorem normal_pSubgroup_le_pCore ...theorem map_pCore_le_of_surjective ...I expect Option A, the intersection of Sylows, to align better with the official problem statement’s “intersection of all p-Sylow subgroups” phrasing.Option B may make maximality easier. The coding agent should implement both behind separate namespaces:lean namespace PcoreViaSylow namespace PcoreViaSup Then the cluster should compile the same theorem list against both. Keep the definition with fewer downstream coercion and normality headaches; delete the other.

The main interface should be definition-independent:lean theorem pair_mem_twoCore_iff_pair_normalClosure_is_twoGroup {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] {x y : G} :x ∈ twoCore G ∧ y ∈ twoCore G ↔ IsPGroup 2 (Subgroup.normalClosure ({x, y} : Set G)) := ...This theorem is what makes Proposition 2.3 formal: for a surjective finite marking, the pro-2 normal-closure condition is equivalent to the two images lying in O₂(G). The manuscript makes exactly this finite epimorphism equivalence after Theorem 1.2.

5. The structural lemma that replaces case-hammering

The next theorem is the central Turn 2 target:lean theorem admissible_subdirect {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι]{G : ι → Type*} [∀ i, Group (G i)] [∀ i, Fintype (G i)](q : ∀ i, Marking (G i))(h : ∀ i, Admissible (q i)) :Admissible (diagonalSubdirectMarking q) := ...

The proof decomposes uniformly:

Relations:eval_pi for the Expr language.

Generation:diagonalSubdirect is defined as the closure of the diagonal generator tuple.

Wild pro-2 condition:the normal closure of x₀,x₁ in the diagonal subgroup maps into ∏ᵢ O₂(Gᵢ);a subgroup of a finite product of 2-groups is a 2-group;use pair_mem_twoCore_iff_pair_normalClosure_is_twoGroup.This is Lemma 2.1 of the manuscript in Lean form. It also prepares the cofinality proof in Lemma 2.2, where finite intersections of kernels are replaced by one admissible subdirect quotient. The manuscript’s proof strategy is explicitly to construct Γ_A from admissible finite quotients, prove finite epimorphism semantics, then reconstruct from epimorphism counts.

6. Coding-agent work orders for Turn 2 Give the GPT-5.5-Pro coding agent only narrow tasks. Do not ask it to invent the formalization architecture; use the theorem interfaces above.Work order A: Omega2Residue.lean

Prompt:Implement omega2Residue for n > 0 as the CRT idempotent selecting the 2-primary part.Prove:1. omega2Residue_mod_twoPart 2. omega2Residue_mod_oddPart 3. omega2Residue_compat_of_dvd Do not import group theory. Use only Nat/ZMod/Chinese remainder tools already in mathlib.Return only Lean code.

Run 20 variants in parallel:

ZMod CRT route;Nat.find route;Int.ModEq route;ZMod plus coercion route.Keep the variant where omega2Residue_compat_of_dvd is shortest.Work order B: Omega2Power.lean

Prompt:Given omega2Residue_compat_of_dvd, define powOmega2 for finite group elements.Prove map_powOmega2, odd-order vanishing, 2-power-order identity, and idempotence.Use orderOf and orderOf_map_dvd if available; otherwise identify the mathlib theorem with #check probes.Do not touch Q2 words.

Cluster fan-out should focus on finding exact names for:lean orderOf orderOf_dvd_of_pow_eq_one MonoidHom.map_pow orderOf_map_dvd pow_eq_pow_mod_orderOf Work order C: Expr.lean and Q2Words.lean

Prompt:Implement GExpr, eval, eval_map, eval_pi.Then define the exact Q2 words from the manuscript using right conjugation x^g = g⁻¹*x*g and commutator x⁻¹*y⁻¹*x*y.Do not prove any presentation theorem.

Acceptance test:lean

#check Q2Word.tameRelator

#check Q2Word.wildRelator

#check GExpr.eval_map

#check GExpr.eval_pi Work order D: TwoCore.lean

Prompt:Implement twoCore for finite groups.Prove normality, p-group property, maximality among normal 2-subgroups, functoriality under surjective homomorphisms, and normal-closure equivalence for one and two generators.Try the Sylow-intersection definition first; if coercions become unmanageable, try supremum of normal 2-subgroups.Run two isolated namespaces in parallel and compare proof burden.Work order E: Admissible.lean and Subdirect.lean

Prompt:Define Admissible q as generation + two relators + x0,x1 in twoCore.Prove admissible_map_surjective and admissible_subdirect.Use GExpr.eval_map/eval_pi; do not unfold the Q2 wild word except through eval.This is the first meaningful integration test of Stage A.

7. Remote compile strategy for this sprint Use the 100 CPUs as a theorem-selection engine.

Run this matrix:20 jobs: omega2Residue definitions and CRT proofs 15 jobs: powOmega2 orderOf compatibility proofs 20 jobs: pCore definition variants and maximality lemmas 15 jobs: Expr.eval_map / eval_pi proof styles 20 jobs: admissible_map_surjective / admissible_subdirect variants 10 jobs: import minimization and #check theorem-name probes Each job should be one theorem or one tiny file. The job payload should include:JSON{ "file": "Q2Presentation/Foundation/Omega2Power.lean","theorem": "map_powOmega2","imports_hash": "...","statement": "...","allowed_dependencies": [ "omega2Residue_compat_of_dvd","orderOf facts","MonoidHom.map_pow"]}

The pass/fail collector should store:

Lean version / mathlib commit imports proof term error message goal state at failure elapsed time

A theorem graduates from proof-lab to main-clean only when:lean

#print axioms theorem_name contains no custom axiom and no sorry. Turn 1 already made this a final acceptance condition.

8. First regression tests Regression tests should be executable but non-authoritative. They should protect conventions, not replace proofs.

Add a file:Q2Presentation/Test/FoundationSmoke.lean

Tests:lean -- Right conjugation convention:example [Group G] (x g : G) :rconj x g = g⁻¹ * x * g := rfl -- Commutator convention:example [Group G] (x y : G) :comm x y = x⁻¹ * y⁻¹ * x * y := rfl -- Odd-order ω₂-power vanishes:example {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] {g : G}(h : Odd (orderOf g)) :powOmega2 g = 1 :=powOmega2_eq_one_of_odd_order h -- 2-power-order ω₂-power is identity:example {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G] {g : G}(h : ∃ n, orderOf g = 2^n) :powOmega2 g = g :=powOmega2_eq_self_of_two_power_order h Later, Appendix C’s integer 40491355905 mod 85667662080 can be tested against omega2Residue Mver, but it must stay in Test, not in the proof tree. The manuscript explicitly separates the machine-readable serialization from the mathematical proof.

9. What Turn 2 should not do yet Do not start local Tate duality, Demushkin theory, Hilbert symbols, or the boundary-framed induction yet. Those are real gaps, but they are downstream of a clean finite quotient API. Turn 1 identified those local Q₂ components as the largest unresolved interfaces.Do not formalize a general theory of profinite presentations yet. Turn 1’s central design choice remains correct: define Γ_A through admissible finite quotients and finite epimorphism semantics, rather than building a general free profinite presentation API.Do not simplify the wild word by hand in multiple files. Put the word in Q2Words.lean, prove expression functoriality once, and use that everywhere.

10. Turn 2 completion criterion

Turn 2 is successful when these compile in the remote backend:lean

#check Q2Presentation.Gen

#check Q2Presentation.powOmega2

#check Q2Presentation.map_powOmega2

#check Q2Presentation.twoCore

#check Q2Presentation.twoCore_normal

#check Q2Presentation.pair_mem_twoCore_iff_pair_normalClosure_is_twoGroup

#check Q2Presentation.Q2Word.tameRelator

#check Q2Presentation.Q2Word.wildRelator

#check Q2Presentation.Admissible

#check Q2Presentation.admissible_map_surjective

#check Q2Presentation.admissible_subdirect This creates the formal base for the candidate construction. Stage B can then define the inverse system of admissible finite quotients and prove the Lean version of Proposition 2.3: epimorphisms from Γ_A to a finite group are exactly generating quadruples satisfying the two relations and the O₂(G) condition. The manuscript makes this finite-quotient semantics the bridge from the presentation to the later epimorphism-count proof.

ESTABLISHED: Turn 2 refines Stage A into a concrete compile-first foundation: elementwise finite ω₂, expression-level word evaluation, twoCore, admissible markings, quotient preservation, and finite subdirect closure.

GAP: No Stage A Lean code has actually been compiled in this conversation; the riskiest immediate choices are the exact omega2Residue implementation and the best mathlib-compatible definition of twoCore.

NEXT: Run the parallel Omega2Residue / TwoCore definition bake-off on the remote Lean backend, promote the shortest passing theorem interfaces to main-clean, then assemble Admissible and admissible_subdirect.[tag: STAGE-A-FOUNDATION]
